questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The rise of a liquid inside a capillary tube is dependent on
options: the length
the material
the inner radius of the tube
the outer radius
questions.solution:
The capillary rise $h$ is given by Jurin's law:

$h = \dfrac{2T \cos \theta}{\rho g r}$

where $T$ is the surface tension of the liquid, $ heta$ is the angle of contact, $ ho$ is the density of the liquid, $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity, and $r$ is the inner radius of the capillary tube.

The angle of contact $ heta$ depends on the nature of the liquid and the material of the capillary tube.

Thus, the rise of the liquid depends on the material of the tube and its inner radius. It is independent of the length and the outer radius of the tube.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The contact angle formed between a solid and a liquid depends on
options: the material of the solid
the mass of the solid
the shape of the solid
the material of the liquid
questions.solution:
The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is determined by the adhesive forces between the liquid and solid molecules and the cohesive forces between the liquid molecules.

Therefore, it depends on the nature of the materials involved, which means it depends on the material of the solid and the material of the liquid.

It is independent of the mass and shape of the solid.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: While determining the Young modulus of a metal wire, a student plots a graph from the recorded readings but omits the axis labels. As shown in the figure, the quantities on the $X$ and $Y$-axes could respectively be
options: length increased and the square of the weight hung
weight hung and length increased
stress applied and strain developed
stress applied and length increased
questions.solution:
The given graph is a straight line passing through the origin, which implies that the quantity on the Y-axis is directly proportional to the quantity on the X-axis ($Y \propto X$).

Young's modulus is given by $Y = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{W/A}{\Delta L/L}$, where $W$ is the weight hung, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $\Delta L$ is the increase in length, and $L$ is the original length.

Let us analyze the given options:

For option (1): X-axis = $\Delta L$, Y-axis = $W^2$. Since $W \propto \Delta L$, we have $W^2 \propto (\Delta L)^2$. This would result in a parabolic graph, not a straight line.

For option (2): X-axis = $W$, Y-axis = $\Delta L$. We know $\Delta L = \left(\dfrac{L}{YA}\right) W$. Since $L, Y,$ and $A$ are constants for a given wire, $\Delta L \propto W$. This gives a straight line passing through the origin.

For option (3): X-axis = Stress, Y-axis = Strain. We know $\text{Strain} = \left(\dfrac{1}{Y}\right) \text{Stress}$. Since $Y$ is constant, $\text{Strain} \propto \text{Stress}$. This gives a straight line passing through the origin.

For option (4): X-axis = Stress, Y-axis = $\Delta L$. We know $\Delta L = L \times \text{Strain} = \left(\dfrac{L}{Y}\right) \text{Stress}$. Since $L$ and $Y$ are constants, $\Delta L \propto \text{Stress}$. This gives a straight line passing through the origin.

Thus, the quantities on the X and Y-axes could be those in options (2), (3), and (4).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At a location where $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$, a solid sphere falls through air with a terminal velocity of $20 \text{ m/s}$. The sphere is subsequently brought into a gravity-free hall containing air at the same pressure, where it is pushed downward at a speed of $20 \text{ m/s}$.
options: The magnitude of its acceleration will decrease over time.
Its initial acceleration will be $9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$ directed upward.
Its initial acceleration will be $9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$ directed downward.
It will eventually come to a halt.
questions.solution:
When the sphere falls with a terminal velocity of $20 \text{ m/s}$, the net force acting on it is zero. Neglecting the small buoyant force in air, the upward drag force $F_d$ equals the downward gravitational force $mg$.

$F_d = mg = m \times 9.8$

In the gravity-free hall, $g = 0$, so the weight of the sphere is zero. When the sphere is pushed downward at $20 \text{ m/s}$, the same drag force $F_d$ acts on it in the upward direction, opposite to its velocity. The initial acceleration is given by:

$a = \dfrac{F_d}{m} = \dfrac{mg}{m} = g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$ directed upward.

Since the drag force depends on the velocity of the sphere, as the velocity decreases, the drag force also decreases. Consequently, the magnitude of its acceleration will decrease over time.

The drag force continuously opposes the motion, reducing the velocity until the sphere eventually comes to a halt.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The properties of a surface differ from those of the bulk liquid because the surface molecules
options: experience a net force in one direction
acquire charge due to collisions with air molecules
are smaller than the other molecules
encounter different types of molecules within their range of influence
questions.solution:
Molecules deep inside a liquid are surrounded by similar molecules on all sides, making the net intermolecular force on them zero.

Molecules at the surface are not completely surrounded by liquid molecules. They have liquid molecules below them and molecules of a different medium (like air or vapor) above them. Thus, they encounter different types of molecules within their range of influence.

Due to the difference in the number and type of molecules above and below, the cohesive downward force from the liquid is greater than the adhesive upward force from the medium above. As a result, surface molecules experience a net inward force (in one direction) towards the bulk of the liquid.

Therefore, surface molecules experience a net force in one direction and encounter different types of molecules within their range of influence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Upon dipping a capillary tube into a liquid, the liquid level neither rises nor falls within the capillary.
options: The angle of contact may be $90^\circ$.
The surface tension of the liquid must be zero.
The surface tension of the liquid may be zero.
The angle of contact must be $90^\circ$.
questions.solution:
The height of the liquid column in a capillary tube is given by Jurin's law:

$h = \dfrac{2T \cos \theta}{\rho g r}$

where $T$ is the surface tension, $\theta$ is the angle of contact, $\rho$ is the density of the liquid, $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity, and $r$ is the radius of the capillary tube.

Given that the liquid level neither rises nor falls, $h = 0$.

$\dfrac{2T \cos \theta}{\rho g r} = 0$

Since $\rho$, $g$, and $r$ are non-zero constants for a given setup, we must have:

$T \cos \theta = 0$

This implies that either $T = 0$ or $\cos \theta = 0$.

If $\cos \theta = 0$, then the angle of contact $\theta = 90^\circ$.

If $T = 0$, then the surface tension of the liquid is zero.

Thus, the angle of contact may be $90^\circ$ or the surface tension may be zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vertical tube with a semicircular cross section contains a liquid, as shown in the figure. The contact angle is zero. The ratio of the surface tension force acting on the curved part to the surface tension force on the flat part is
questions.solution:
Let the radius of the semicircular cross-section of the tube be $R$.

The surface tension force acting on any part of the wall is given by $F = T \times l \times \cos\theta$, where $T$ is the surface tension of the liquid, $l$ is the length of the line of contact, and $\theta$ is the contact angle.

Given that the contact angle $\theta = 0^{\circ}$, we have $\cos\theta = 1$. Thus, the force is simply proportional to the length of contact, $F = T \times l$.

For the curved part, the length of the line of contact is the arc length of the semicircle:
$l_c = \pi R$
Therefore, the surface tension force on the curved part is:
$F_c = T \times \pi R$

For the flat part, the length of the line of contact is the diameter of the semicircle:
$l_f = 2R$
Therefore, the surface tension force on the flat part is:
$F_f = T \times 2R$

The ratio of the surface tension force acting on the curved part to that on the flat part is:
$\dfrac{F_c}{F_f} = \dfrac{T \times \pi R}{T \times 2R} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$
